feed.title: Combined summary - Compact Isogeny PQC can replace HD wallets, key-tweaking, silent payments

entry.title: Combined summary - Compact Isogeny PQC can replace HD wallets, key-tweaking, silent payments
entry.summary: Isogeny-based cryptography (IBC) represents a significant evolution in the field of encryption, offering compact and efficient solutions particularly relevant in the age of quantum computing threats. These cryptographic methods utilize isogenies and endomorphisms to map points between or within elliptic curves, presenting a smaller footprint in terms of key and signature sizes compared to lattice-based schemes. This efficiency makes IBC especially attractive for applications requiring reduced data overhead such as in blockchain technologies. The foundational challenges in this area, like the supersingular isogeny path problem (SIPP) and the endomorphism ring problem (ERP), are crucial for ensuring the security and feasibility of cryptographic constructions using isogenies. Understanding these problems and their interconnections lays the groundwork for developing secure cryptographic protocols that can potentially resist quantum attacks. The practical applications of isogeny-based methods extend beyond traditional encryption tasks. They are increasingly considered for implementation in scenarios such as signature schemes, key derivation processes, and advanced cryptographic proofs like zero-knowledge proofs of knowledge (zkpok). Zkpok, which leverages the homomorphic properties of isogenies, underscores the broader utility of these mappings in cryptographic systems. Dual isogenies play a pivotal role here, functioning similarly to inverses in discrete logarithm-based systems and aiding in the formulation of soundness proofs within cryptographic protocols. This aspect is vital for ensuring the reliability and security of encryption methods based on isogenies. Moreover, recent discussions and research into the integration of isogeny-based cryptography within more complex cryptographic systems such as multisignature schemes highlight the ongoing innovation and exploration in the field. The development of methods like PRISM and the exploration of threshold signatures using isogenies suggest new directions for collaborative cryptographic operations, which are essential for enhancing security in distributed systems like blockchains. The potential for incorporating features such as rerandomization, aggregation, and batch verification into IBC protocols could further solidify their applicability and robustness in various cryptographic applications. Nonetheless, the adoption and adaptation of isogeny-based cryptography come with challenges, notably in terms of compatibility with existing cryptographic infrastructures and the inherent complexity of isogenies compared to classical elliptic curve operations. The security assumptions underlying these new cryptographic constructions also warrant careful scrutiny, particularly concerning the hardness of problems like SIPP and ERP and the potential for future cryptanalytic breakthroughs. As researchers continue to address these issues and refine isogeny-based cryptographic techniques, the engagement with community resources and ongoing discourse, such as those found at [DelvingBitcoin.org](https://delvingbitcoin.org/t/post-quantum-hd-wallets-silent-payments-key-aggregation-and-threshold-signatures/1854) and [Conduition.io](https://conduition.io/cryptography/isogenies-intro/), remains critical for fostering wider understanding and implementation of these advanced cryptographic methods.
